steps.title: Fastest path: mark the figure first
steps.content: For coordinate-circle questions, read the center and radius from the equation or graph the circle as a check. For diagram facts, mark equal radii, diameters, tangents, and arcs before doing algebra.

steps.title: Set up
steps.content:
Two chords AC and BE intersect at D inside the circle. The intersecting chords theorem gives $AD \cdot DC = BD \cdot DE$. Since $BD = DE = \sqrt{398}$, the right side is $\sqrt{398} \cdot \sqrt{398} = 398$, so $AD \cdot DC = 398$.

Because $BD = DE$, point D is the midpoint of chord BE. The perpendicular from the center to a chord bisects it, so AC — which is perpendicular to BE at D — passes through the center, making AC a diameter: $AD + DC = 201$.

steps.title: Solve
steps.content:
Let $AD = t$, so $DC = 201 - t$. Then:
$$t(201 - t) = 398 \implies t^2 - 201t + 398 = 0$$
$$t = \frac{201 \pm \sqrt{40401 - 1592}}{2} = \frac{201 \pm \sqrt{38809}}{2} = \frac{201 \pm 197}{2}$$
So $t = 199$ or $t = 2$.

Since $AB \lt BC$, point B is closer to A, meaning D is closer to A: $AD = 2$, $DC = 199$.
$$r = \frac{DC}{AD} = \frac{199}{2}$$
